headline: Greece's Tiny Satellites Spot Wildfires Before They Spread
meta_description: Greece deploys nanosatellites to detect wildfires at just four meters across, catching fires hours earlier than traditional methods.
summary_short: Four shoebox-sized satellites now scan Greece hourly for wildfires, detecting them at a quarter the size conventional satellites can spot.
body:
In May, Greece became the first country to launch satellites designed specifically for wildfire response. The four OroraTech nanosatellites—each smaller than a carry-on bag—orbit overhead scanning for thermal signatures that reveal danger before flames spread beyond control.

Conventional satellites can't detect a fire until it reaches the size of a cruise ship. These new ones catch them at just four meters across. That difference means hours of precious time in remote terrain, where wildfires can grow unchecked while no one watches.

Here's how it works: each satellite captures thermal images and feeds them to AI models that analyze data in four-by-four-meter grid cells. The artificial intelligence filters out false alarms from solar panels, factory rooftops, and sun-warmed rock before alerting emergency services. When a fire is confirmed, incident commanders receive location, size, and intensity instantly.

The system updates every hour with no gaps in coverage—critical during Greece's brutal summer months, when the country faces some of Europe's worst wildfire seasons. Fire Brigade Commander Zisoula Dasiou emphasized that hourly monitoring matters most in isolated valleys and national parks, where small fires can vanish from sight until they become catastrophic.

With all four satellites now operational and EU-funded, Greece's emergency responders have gained an early-warning system that transforms how they fight nature's fiercest threat.
